item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was about to ask why the guy who wrote the diary was unaffected by the memetic hazard, but then I realised: he was one of the few people who are immune to the meme's effects.

item.title: Speculation...
item.content:encoded:
I'm probably not the first, nor the last, to say this, but when reading the journal entry, I figured, "there has to be some sort of connection to something in history" when the year was blocked out. At first, I thought it was Columbus, but the timeline didn't match up. But then, the "big story", and natives, that gave me clues, but the first people to circumnavigate the world…

HOLY SHIT IT'S MAGELLAN!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm wondering if anyone was reminded of Bermuda Triangle. That's the only thing in my mind right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My god, it's Magellan!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I immediately thought of Event Horizon, because it reads as some kind of Event Horizon of the seas. Creepy as hell. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's currently up to author discretion, I think. So if the author has "amnesiac" and they don't want to change it, then don't change it for them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are we still changing "amnesiac" to "amnestic"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two things from the recovered logbook:
1. Why did you bother redacting the country? How could it be anything else but Spain, considering the era and use of the Spanish language?
2. It says that there was just one pristine white sail left of the flagship, but it says earlier that all ships returning from the scp have tattered, destroyed sails.

I live this scp, reminds me of some kind of twisted, awful version of the last straight path to the west from Tolkein's silmarillion

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was good, but it left me wanting more in a bad way. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article is good, but what I really liked was the mariner's diary. Creepy…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This skip is utterly fascinating. Taking a common myth of past people's understanding of science and turning into something pretty gosh-darned scary is fucking fantastic. Not only that, but the Chief's way of trying to get across the idea of memetic infection, and his tribe's way of getting around it, was pretty great. This is a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm new here, and this is my first comment. I have to say, I really like this one. The quantity of information combines nicely with the relatively small amount of actual data. The result is a creepy tension that makes you feel that each new thing you learn about the SCP somehow makes its true nature even hazier. I totally agree with Reese Sparrow above; we aren't even really sure whether the "madness" lies within the ships' crews or within the islanders…

My favorite line: "Forgive me, Captain, but I no longer want to know what you saw beyond the edge of the Earth." Chilling. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
THIS^
One thing I loved about this article is the fact that its totally possible that this "memetic infection" is nonexistent and superstition has people killing returning survivors. Or maybe the memetic infection is in the people on the island, not the people returning themselves. at least thats how I read it. /shrug upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"A small number of subjects appear to be immune to these effects, though it is currently not understood why."

I loved this line. Particularly because you didn't need to put it in at all.

Great stuff, and congrats on the featured article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I had the same reaction to this when I first read it (even though I still liked it), but the time passed the more I realized I had come to absolutely love it. I'm not the only one to react that way, either. Something about the article…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
oh look, it is everything I love in an SCP

For me, the scariest thing about all this is that the people who come back might not actually have anything wrong with them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kept wondering how the rest of the article would live up to the ridiculous (from the Foundation's "practical" standpoint, not the actual writing of them) containment procedures. And in my opinion it SERIOUSLY delivers. Cursed words bearing "knowledge from beyond the edge of the world." Holy shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I enjoyed it. It was a little bit 'meh' for a featured skip, especially such a long one. I think it could use a little bit more of an interesting hook, diving more into the memetic hazard. I think it could really do with a minor glimpse of what lies on the other side… maybe a recovered log from a ship that actually went over or such.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember reading this one before I became a member of this site, and didn't recall its name or number until it came up on the front page. The logs really turn this one from a 'good' SCP into a 'great' one.

Happily upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know it's a bit of a late reply, but no, the article itself isn't a memetic hazard. The memetic hazard here is specifically the information conveyed by people who have passed through SCP-1372 and returned, presumably regarding the nature of what is "beyond the edge of the world". Anyone who obtains this information, either by direct observation (the people on the returning ships) or by conversation (hearing and comprehending the things the people on the ships or those they affect are saying) comes under the effect and is compelled to seek out other humans and convey the memetic information to them. To "spread the word" as it were.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 because it made me think of a "what about a flat world?" question…

… and the diary thing creeped me out O.O

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't the article be labeled with "memetic hazard"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I disagree with faminepulse about "mystery for the sake of mystery." This is great! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't totally disagree with the comments saying that they wish there was more meat here, and yet I find myself upvoting anyway. The amount of good detail work put into this really helps. I also particularly like the whole 'sail X direction, you go off the edge of the world and come back with maddening knowledge' detail, especially because the compulsion to come isn't irresistable.

+1'ed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You best start believin' in SCPs.

You're in one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh, yeah. This particular memetic contagion requires actual comprehension of the words, so if you can't make out what they're saying, or they speak a language that you don't, you're unaffected.
… though I have to admit the idea for that started from ophite, who helped me tremendously on the anthropological aspects of that interview log. The idea of the tribe having two different languages was totally ophite's, honestly.

Of course, this is the real problem nowadays. The islanders could contain it with their split-language defense. But now English is becoming more and more the lingua franca of the world (not completely, obviously, but in a lot of places people at least have a partial understanding of it). And now people going in are English-speakers…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's well-written, but a bit too long for me to really enjoy it. The containment procedure for one seems a little over-detailed, but I can't think of any specific changes that wouldn't call for a larger re-write to remain consistent with the effect.

On the plus side, I absolutely love the conversation with the chief. The islanders established containment protocols on this thing generations ago, and they have a better defense against memetic hazards than the Foundation uses today. "Sorry Mr. Mind-Raper, I'd love to spread your message of virulent madness and gibbering horror form beyond reality, but no hablo ingles."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see what you're saying, but I'm not quite sure what more I can say that wouldn't make it completely overt. Though keep in mind, said incident is the reason that we don't allow aircraft within a kilometer of this thing, so… hopefully it makes a bit more sense from there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it a little better after reading the interview, but I'd love to see more information on the "incident" that caused such trouble. I know the whole "not knowing can be better than knowing" thing can sometimes make something better, but in this case, I just feel like it's missing that last little morsel to put it over the edge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is mystery for the sake of mystery and the hook isn't really strong enough to pull me in. I have to downvote on concept but everything else is alright.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Part of me wants to dismiss this as "the Devil's Triangle that spits out ghost ships filled with magical ponies", but this is well written and very interesting, and that last part is very important for a lengthy article. The interview log filled in a lot of the gaps as well. Quite creative, well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … I hadn't even considered the possibility of non-human beings entering… I sort of doubt it would do much, though, the contagion at least at present appears to be only transmitted vocally. But yeah, THAT thing being in the area beyond the edge is probably Not A Good Thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God, that makes sense, but it's still bothering me too much to upvote. >:|

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was worth the long read. I got the thought of what would happen if SCP-835 went into this strange area…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pauli's got the general idea. This thing has an unknown, non-biological contagion associated with anything that emerges, and if you give that contagion's carrier a plane, that's bad. Also, this object can't be moved, and its containment procedures necessitate its own armed containment site with over 1,000 personnel devoted solely to it. That's a pretty involved and complex containment procedure, even if it does boil down to "don't let people go across the line".

One more thing, there are ships that have emerged that don't correspond to any recorded ship lost from crossing SCP-1372. That means there's a possibility that there are ships that weren't built on Earth emerging from it.

I actually did waffle a lot between Euclid and Keter on this, but eventually decided to err on the side of "caution" on the grounds that there are aspects of this thing that are totally unknown, it has the potential for serious damage if it does slip past ACS-70 somehow, and it requires a very involved method of containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
AYYYE we be good and lost now. For sure, one must be lost to get to that which can't be found!
…. Sorry. Pirates of the Caribbean marathon the other day, and this reminded me no small bit of the trip to Davey Jones' Locker. I know of course that wasn't the exact thing you were going for, just the general concept, and I must say you did a better job than expected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
in case a plane comes out.

especially a plane with a radio…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I might be missing something obvious, but why is this Keter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not the weirdest thing the Foundation's found - heck, we've got a couple of topological twists, a handful of temporal disruptions, and some links to alternate universes. And a wading pool that teleports people into space. And then there's 343, who may or may not be God. I like the way this one's written, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They unveil a vaguely described memetic contagion. It's really not fleshed out, and the lack of meat just isn't satisfying…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Earth isn't flat. It's just a sphere with a small topological oddity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, the Earth's a sphere. Except if you cross this one particular small segment of a geodesic, and then it's got an edge. Everywhere else it obeys normal topology. Also, it's less atmospheric than… well, there is some form of non-biological contagion that O5 has determined should get the [DATA EXPUNGED] treatment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i think it's more that the earth is a sphere… with a one-way corner to crazy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please tell me this article isn't seriously saying that in the Foundation-verse, the Earth is flat. Because that would be dumb. Besides that, even with the supplementary interview, it's pretty unclear what this does. It's atmospheric, but not much more than that. Also, the amnesiacs thing is silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Give the supplementary documents another read. They actually unveil pretty much everything that's hiding under that [DATA EXPUNGED], hence why the second one is classified 4/1372. Though I am considering tinkering a bit with that particular sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When boarded, all instances of SCP-1372-1 were found to contain [DATA EXPUNGED]

I feel so cockblocked. The entire article makes me feel even more angry about it. Long article, but there's not enough bang for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn it, I knew the world was flat! Discworld, here I come! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, a little lengthy for my tastes but still well enough.
